title: Merging: Bringing a Branch's Work Back Home
learningObjectives: Merge a finished branch back into main
Distinguish a fast-forward merge from a merge commit, and explain why the distinction exists
Read a merge commit's history correctly using git log --graph
Decide when a branch's work is actually ready to merge versus needs more testing first
whyItMatters: Your threshold experiment worked. It needs to actually reach main so the rest of the Digital Twin benefits from it — an experiment that stays isolated forever provides no value.
capstoneContribution: The mechanism by which every validated improvement — a better forecasting model, a safer battery policy — actually lands in the AI Energy OS's trusted main line, rather than staying stranded on an experimental branch forever.

problem.summary: A validated change exists only on a branch main hasn't benefited from at all.
problem.content: Your `new-thresholds` branch has a change you've validated and want. It currently only exists on that branch — main hasn't benefited from it at all.

motivation.summary: An isolated experiment provides zero value until it's merged.
motivation.content: An experiment that stays isolated forever provides zero value. Merging is the step that turns 'I tried something on a branch' into 'the project actually improved.'

theory.summary: A fast-forward merge just moves main's pointer; a merge commit joins two diverged lines.
theory.content: `git merge new-thresholds` while on main integrates that branch's commits into main. If main hasn't moved since the branch was created, Git performs a fast-forward merge — simply moving main's pointer forward, since no real merge is needed. If main has moved (other commits happened on main meanwhile), Git creates a merge commit with two parents, recording that two lines of history came together. `git log --graph --oneline` visualizes this history, including merge commits, as an actual graph rather than a flat list.

visualExplanation.summary: Fast-forward slides a pointer forward; a merge commit joins two diverging lines with two parent arrows.
visualExplanation.content: A fast-forward merge (main's pointer simply slides forward to where the branch already is) is contrasted against a merge commit (two diverging lines rejoin at a new commit with two parent arrows).

interactiveDemo.summary: Merge in two scenarios and watch the resulting graph differ.
interactiveDemo.content: Merge a branch in two different scenarios — one where main hasn't moved (fast-forward) and one where it has (merge commit) — and watch the resulting commit graph differ between the two.

guidedCoding.summary: Follow along: merge a validated branch back into main.
guidedCoding.content:
```bash
git switch main
git merge new-thresholds
git log --graph --oneline
```

independentCoding.summary: Merge your own branch and identify which merge type occurred.
independentCoding.content: Merge your own experimental branch from the previous lesson back into main, then use `git log --graph` to confirm whether Git performed a fast-forward or created a merge commit, and explain which one happened and why.

debugging.summary: Diagnose an unexpected merge outcome by checking whether main had moved.
debugging.content: A learner expected a merge commit but got a fast-forward instead, and is confused why. Walk through checking whether main had moved since the branch was created to explain the actual outcome.

reflection.summary: Fast-forward is preferred when history doesn't need to record a real join.
reflection.content: Why does Git prefer a fast-forward merge when it's possible, rather than always creating a merge commit?

teachBack.summary: Explain fast-forward vs. merge commit using your own repository's graph.
teachBack.content: Explain to someone the difference between a fast-forward merge and a merge commit, using the actual `git log --graph` output from your own repository as the example.

miniProject.summary: Merge every validated branch into a clean, explainable main history.
miniProject.content: Merge every validated experimental branch you've created so far back into main, ending with a clean `git log --graph` history you can explain commit by commit.

portfolioEvidence.summary: main's history is now a real record of validated, integrated improvements.
portfolioEvidence.content: Your main branch's history is now the actual record of which Digital Twin improvements have been validated and integrated, as opposed to just attempted.

labs.title: Integrate the Branch
labs.description: Sandboxed exercise providing two scenarios (main unchanged vs. main advanced) requiring the learner to merge correctly and correctly identify which merge type Git performed in each.

simulations.title: Merge Type Visualizer
simulations.description: Side-by-side animation contrasting a fast-forward merge against a merge commit as the same branch is merged under different main-history conditions.

flashcards.front: What determines whether git merge produces a fast-forward or a merge commit?
flashcards.back: Whether main has moved since the branch was created. If main is unchanged, Git simply fast-forwards its pointer; if main has new commits, Git creates a merge commit joining both histories.

flashcards.front: How many parent commits does a merge commit have, and what does that represent?
flashcards.back: Two — one from each branch being joined, recording that two independent lines of history came together at that point.

rubric: Correctly identifies whether a given merge was a fast-forward or a merge commit, and explains why
Merged history is clean and reflects only intended, validated changes
Can read and narrate a git log --graph output commit by commit

references.title: Pro Git — Git Branching: Basic Branching and Merging
